paintings.title: Akbar Taming the Mad Elephant Hawa'i (Akbarnama)
paintings.medium: opaque watercolour and gold on paper
paintings.story: The young emperor Akbar rides the notoriously fierce elephant Hawa'i in mid-rampage across a bridge of boats — one of the most dramatic illustrations of the Akbarnama, Akbar's official biography by Abu'l Fazl. Basawan designed the composition, with the colouring supplied by Chitra, in the collaborative practice of the imperial workshop where Basawan often acted as designer with a second artist adding colour. His use of portraiture in the Akbarnama illustrations is seen as an innovation in Indian art.
funFacts: The Victoria and Albert Akbarnama contains 116 miniature paintings, thought to have been illustrated between c. 1592 and 1594 by at least forty-nine different artists of Akbar's workshop.
Akbar's historiographer Abu'l Fazl wrote of Basawan: 'In designing and portrait painting and colouring and painting illusionistically... he became unrivalled in the world.'

paintings.title: Akbar Visits the Tomb of Khwaja Mu'in ad-Din Chishti at Ajmer
paintings.medium: opaque watercolour and gold on paper
paintings.story: Akbar pays homage at the shrine of the great Chishti Sufi saint at Ajmer, a pilgrimage the emperor made repeatedly and which the Akbarnama records among the defining images of his piety. Basawan was one of only four artists singled out for comment in the Ain-i-Akbari, alongside Sayyid Ali, Abd al-Samad and Daswanth. He was known by his contemporaries as a skilled colorist and keen observer of human nature.
funFacts: Basawan came under the influence of the Persian master Abd al-Samad at Akbar's court.
His son Manohar Das succeeded him as a court painter, and the family still lives in their heritage home in Kala Mahal, Agra.

paintings.title: Alexander Visits the Sage Plato in his Mountain Cave
paintings.medium: ink, opaque watercolour and gold on paper
paintings.location: Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York
paintings.story: Alexander the Great humbly seeks out the sage Plato in his mountain cave, in a folio from the Khamsa (Quintet) of the poet Amir Khusrau Dihlavi illuminated for Akbar's court. Basawan was noted for his exploration of space, the delineation of his backgrounds, the strength of his colours, and the strong, moving characterizations of his subjects — all visible in the craggy hermitage and the deferential king.
funFacts: Basawan was one of the first Indian artists interested in Western techniques, inspired by European paintings brought to Akbar's court by Jesuit missionaries.
Over 100 paintings are attributed to Basawan, most of them illustrations for manuscripts.

paintings.title: A Muslim Pilgrim Learns a Lesson in Piety from a Brahman
paintings.medium: ink, opaque watercolour and gold on paper
paintings.location: Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York
paintings.story: In this folio from the Khamsa of Amir Khusrau Dihlavi, a Muslim pilgrim receives a lesson in devotion from a Hindu Brahman — a subject that spoke to the spirit of religious dialogue cultivated at Akbar's court. Basawan's keen observation of human nature, remarked on by his contemporaries, animates the encounter between the two men of faith.
funFacts: Though Western influence appears in his strong contrasts of light and shade, it is never predominant in Basawan's work.
Basawan was a Yadav from Uttar Pradesh who rose to become one of the most esteemed painters of Akbar's imperial workshop.

paintings.title: Jain Ascetic Walking Along a Riverbank
paintings.story: A sky-clad Jain ascetic strides along a riverbank, observed with the frank, humane curiosity that made Basawan the great character-painter of the Mughal workshop. The receding landscape behind him shows the exploration of space and delineation of background for which he was noted, absorbing lessons from the European prints that Jesuit missionaries had brought to Akbar's court without ever surrendering the Indian idiom.
funFacts: Basawan's earliest mention is found in an illustrated Tutinama of c. 1556–61.
Despite his significance to Mughal art, Basawan is little known outside academia in the Western world.

paintings.title: The Flight of the Simurgh
paintings.medium: opaque watercolour and gold on paper
paintings.story: The mythical simurgh, the great bird of Persian legend, soars with a figure clinging beneath its wings in one of Basawan's most fantastical designs. Among the works that can definitely be attributed to Basawan are illustrations for the Razmnama, the Akbarnama, the Darabnama, the Baharistan of Jami and the Timurnama — the full range of the epics, histories and fables produced for Akbar's library.
funFacts: In many manuscript illustrations Basawan was the designer, collaborating with a second artist who supplied the colour.
The strength of his colours was one of the qualities his contemporaries most admired.

paintings.title: Battle of the Rival Ascetics at Thanesar (Akbarnama)
paintings.medium: opaque watercolour and gold on paper
paintings.story: Rival bands of ascetics clash at Thanesar as the young Akbar looks on, in one of the Akbarnama's most turbulent pages. Basawan's strong, moving characterizations of his subjects fill the melee with distinct individuals rather than types — the innovation of portraiture within narrative illustration that made the Akbarnama a landmark in Indian art.
funFacts: The Akbarnama took its author Abu'l Fazl, one of the Nine Jewels of Akbar's court, seven years to complete.
The V&A bought its Akbarnama in 1896 from Mrs Frances Clarke, whose husband had acquired it on retiring as Commissioner of Oudh.

paintings.title: Assad Ibn Kariba Launches a Night Attack on the Camp of Malik Iraj (Hamzanama)
paintings.medium: ink, opaque watercolour and gold on cloth
paintings.location: Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York
paintings.story: Warriors storm a sleeping camp by night in this folio from the Hamzanama, the enormous illustrated manuscript commissioned by the emperor Akbar about 1562 to narrate the legendary exploits of Hamza, uncle of the Prophet. The stories are extremely fanciful — 'a continuous series of romantic interludes, threatening events, narrow escapes, and violent acts' — and the young Basawan was among the workshop artists who gave them visual form at the very start of his career.
funFacts: The Hamzanama project was so vast that its adventures fill over 45,000 pages in the longest Urdu version of the tales.

Akbar's court chronicler tells us Hamza's adventures were 'represented in twelve volumes, and clever painters made the most astonishing illustrations for no less than one thousand and four hundred passages of the story.'

paintings.title: Tumanba Khan, His Wife, and His Nine Sons (Chingiznama)
paintings.medium: ink, opaque watercolour and gold on paper
paintings.location: Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York
paintings.story: The Mongol patriarch Tumanba Khan sits enthroned with his wife and his nine sons ranged about him, in a folio from the Chingiznama, the Book of Genghis Khan, produced for Akbar around 1596. The Mughals traced their ancestry to the house of Genghis Khan and Timur, and dynastic histories like this one asserted the family's imperial pedigree. It is among the late masterpieces attributed to Basawan, whose activity is documented from about 1556 to 1600.
funFacts: Basawan is described as flourishing between 1580 and 1600, at the height of Akbar's manuscript ateliers.
The 'Mughal' dynasty's very name derives from 'Mongol', the ancestry celebrated in the Chingiznama.
